author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Chapter 10

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let these things suffice you, my beloved Christian brethren of the Catholic Church, so far as the present business is concerned; and if you hold fast to this in Catholic affection, so long as you are one sure flock of the one Shepherd, I am not too much concerned with the abuse that any enemy may lavish on me, your partner in the flock, or, at any rate, your watch-dog, so long as he compels me to bark rather in your defense than in my own.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And yet, if it were necessary for the cause that I should enter on my own defense, I should do so with the greatest brevity and the greatest ease, joining freely with all men in condemning and bearing witness against the whole period of my life before I received the baptism of Christ, so far as relates to my evil passions and my errors, lest, in defending that period, I should seem to be seeking my own glory, not His, who by His grace delivered me even from myself.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Wherefore, when I hear that life of mine abused, in whatever spirit he may be acting who abuses it, I am not so thankless as to be grieved.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: However much he finds fault with any vice of mine, I praise him in the same degree as my physician.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why then should I disturb myself about defending those past and obsolete evils in my life, in respect of which, though Petilianus has said much that is false, he has yet left more that is true unsaid?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But concerning that period of my life which is subsequent to my baptism, to you who know me I speak unnecessarily in telling of those things which might be known to all mankind; but those who know me not ought not to act with such unfairness towards me as to believe Petilianus rather than you concerning me.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if one should not give credence to the panegyrics of a friend, neither should one believe the detraction of an enemy.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: There remain, therefore, those things which are hidden in a man, in which conscience alone can bear testimony, which cannot be a witness before men.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Herein Petilianus says that I am a Manichæan, speaking of the conscience of another man; I, speaking of my own conscience, aver that I am not.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Choose which of us you had sooner believe.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Notwithstanding, since there is not any need even of this short and easy defense on my part, where the question at issue is not concerning the merits of any individual, whoever he may be, but concerning the truth of the whole Church, I have more also to say to any of you, who, being of the party of Donatus, have read the evil words which Petilianus has written about me, which I should not have heard from him if I had had no care about the loss of your salvation; but then I should have been wanting in the bowels of Christian love.
